metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve '4-way classification' wording in the Scope section: either correct it to '5-way classification' (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated) or explicitly state that the LLM is asked to output from all 5 enum values and 'related_distinct'/'unrelated' are LLM-native outputs (not internal downgrade artifacts).
findings.finding: The plan requires the rubric prompt body to encode 'the 4-way classification', but the `relation` enum has 5 values: {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}. An executor writing the prompt cannot determine which four the 'classification' refers to — whether `related_distinct` and `unrelated` are both LLM-emittable outputs or whether one is an internal downgrade artifact not in the LLM's output set.
findings.location: Scope section — 'body MUST encode: the 4-way classification'; What section — relation enum definition
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the 4-way classification' to 'the 5-way classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)' and note that related_distinct and unrelated are both valid LLM outputs, with the aggregation rule downgrading disagreements to related_distinct internally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fixture format for `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` (e.g., JSON with fields `{pair_id, digest_a, digest_b, ground_truth_relation, should_surface: bool}`) so the executor can implement both the fixtures and `test_judge_precision_recall` without design decisions.
findings.finding: The fixture directory `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` is named but its file format, schema, and required fields are not specified. An executor building the ≥12 labeled pairs cannot determine whether these are JSON, YAML, plain-text, or a custom format, nor which fields are required (e.g., pair IDs, digests, ground-truth relation label, expected surface/no-surface outcome).
findings.location: Testing section — 'a LABELED fixture of ≥12 pairs at tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: 'Each fixture file is a JSON object: `{"pair_id": str, "digest_a": str, "digest_b": str, "ground_truth_relation": str|null, "should_surface": bool}`. The FakeRunner maps `(pair_id, ordering)` to a canned `overlap_verdict` defined in the test body.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `test_llm_error_abstain` to assert that a log record at WARNING or ERROR level was emitted (e.g., using `caplog` or a mock logger), so the 'logged' claim in AC4 is actually proven.
findings.finding: AC4 states LLM errors are 'logged' but the proving command `test_llm_error_abstain` is described only as verifying 'no raise' — there is no assertion that logging actually occurred, leaving the 'logged' claim unmeasured.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'LLM error on a candidate → abstain, logged, no raise; ::test_llm_error_abstain'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, update the test description to: 'test_llm_error_abstain (no raise, and caplog asserts a WARNING-or-higher log record containing the candidate ID).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC that runs `overlap_judge.py` against a live (or recorded-replay) LLM call on at least one canonical pair and asserts the expected `relation` in the output, OR explicitly defer this to a named child story (e.g., 'S6-live-eval') with a rationale so the gap is intentional and tracked.
findings.finding: The LLM overlap judge's core value — the rubric prompt (`overlap_judge.md`) that classifies pairs — is exercised exclusively via FakeRunner in all proving commands. No AC exercises the prompt against a live LLM, and the plan's rationale ('no registered scorer exists') does not defer to a named child story or ticket where live validation will occur.
findings.location: Testing section and Acceptance Criteria — all proving commands use FakeRunner; 'Precision/recall check as a PYTEST FIXTURE test (NOT the prompt-eval harness — no registered scorer exists)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'the 4-way classification' in Scope and the 5-value enum in What — either correct 'the 4-way classification' to 'the 5-way classification' or explain which value was intentionally collapsed and update the enum accordingly.
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs that the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the What section defines a 5-value closed enum (unrelated, related_distinct, depends_on, supersedes, duplicates). A prompt author following 'the 4-way classification' instruction would under-specify the enum by one value, creating a cross-section contradiction.
findings.location: What section ('relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}') vs Scope section ('the 4-way classification')
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the 4-way classification' in the Scope section to 'the 5-way classification (unrelated, related_distinct, depends_on, supersedes, duplicates)' to match the What section enum exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies `overlap_verdict.schema.json` is present and validates against the described shape, and that `register_contract('overlap_verdict', ...)` is callable without error.
findings.finding: Two described deliverables — the `overlap_verdict.schema.json` JSON Schema file and the `register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)` call — have no covering acceptance criterion. No AC verifies that the schema file is valid, present, or that contract registration succeeds, leaving these outputs as orphaned deliverables.
findings.location: Scope section ('overlap_verdict schema src/rebar/schemas/overlap_verdict.schema.json + register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)') vs Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a dedicated section in CLAUDE.md / design doc) capturing the dual-ordering position-bias rationale, the both-agree aggregation rule, and why single-ordering cascade is explicitly excluded.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering position-bias mitigation aggregation rule is a novel architectural decision governing correctness of the entire stage-2 judge (both-orderings-must-agree, abstain-downgrades-safe, no single-ordering cascade), but no ADR or design-doc section is planned to capture it.
findings.location: ## What — output schema / AGGREGATION bullet; ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNNN-overlap-judge-dual-ordering.md` with: (1) context — position bias in LLM pairwise judgment; (2) decision — require both A→B and B→A to agree AND cite a shared_artifact; (3) consequences — 2K calls per K candidates, abstain-safe degradation. Reference it from the `## Why` section of this plan and from a docstring in `judge.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope section an explicit step to update the configuration reference doc (README config table or `docs/configuration.md`) with `overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap`, their defaults, env var names, and valid ranges.
findings.finding: Two new user-facing configuration keys (`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap` on `LLMConfig`, env vars `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*`, TOML section `[tool.rebar.llm]`) are introduced but the plan does not mention updating any configuration reference documentation, README, or CHANGELOG.
findings.location: ## Config (LLMConfig, not VerifyConfig)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the maximum value of K (stage-1 candidate cap) and add a concurrency/rate-limit handling note (e.g. semaphore, max-parallel calls) for the 2K LLM fan-out in the What or Config section.
findings.finding: The plan states 'K candidates → 2K judge calls' but never defines an upper bound for K. Without a cap on K, the number of LLM calls per query invocation is unbounded in the spec, making per-query cost and latency unquantifiable.
findings.location: ## What — output schema / 'K candidates → 2K judge calls, flat in corpus size'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Config section: `overlap_candidate_cap` (default e.g. 10, max 20) on LLMConfig bounding K, and note that the 2K calls are dispatched with a configurable concurrency limit (e.g. `overlap_judge_concurrency`, default 4) to respect provider rate limits.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum one retry (with backoff) before abstaining on transient LLM errors, and emit a structured log/metric (e.g. `overlap_judge.abstain_count`, `overlap_judge.error_count`) so operators can detect systematic failure — update the error-handling spec and `::test_llm_error_abstain` to cover the retry path.
findings.finding: LLM errors are immediately treated as abstain with no retry or backoff step. Transient network or provider errors will silently degrade the judge's recall without any recovery attempt, and only 'logged' is specified — no metric, trace, or alert for detecting systematic failure.
findings.location: ## What — output schema / 'Errors: LLM timeout/error/malformed output on a candidate → treated as abstain (dropped), logged'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: on LLM error, attempt one retry with exponential backoff (e.g. 1s, 2s); if still failing, record abstain and increment a `overlap_judge_errors_total` counter (logged at WARN level with candidate id). Update AC to include `::test_llm_error_abstain` verifying the retry occurs before abstain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `register_contract` is idempotent (raises on key conflict OR silently no-ops on re-registration with same schema), state the registration timing (module import vs. explicit init), and note how the entry is retired if the contract is removed in a future story.
findings.finding: `register_contract('overlap_verdict', <builder>)` mutates a global contracts registry, but the plan does not specify whether this call is idempotent under double-import or concurrent module initialization, nor when exactly it executes (import time vs. explicit init).
findings.location: ## Scope / 'register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one boundary test (e.g., `test_empty_candidates` or `test_all_abstain_empty_surface`) asserting the judge returns a clean empty surface list and does not raise when K=0 or all pairs abstain.
findings.finding: No named test covers the empty-input (K=0 candidates) or all-candidates-abstain boundary, which could silently surface an empty list or crash (e.g., division by zero in precision calc, unhandled empty iteration) rather than returning a clean empty surface result.
findings.location: Testing section — unit test list; AC line 'Abstain drops without consuming a slot'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_all_abstain_empty_surface` to `tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py`: configure FakeRunner to return `abstain: true` for every candidate pair and assert the result is `[]` with no exception raised. Optionally add `test_empty_candidates` with an empty input list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` meet the Rule-of-Three bar for `LLMConfig` fields, OR consolidate them into a feature-local config dataclass passed explicitly to `judge.py`; also specify how the float `overlap_conf_threshold` is resolved (a new `_llm_float` helper, or delegation to `_as_float` from `rebar._config_schema`).
findings.finding: `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` are proposed as new `LLMConfig` fields with dedicated `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys, but each has exactly one consuming call-site (the new `judge.py`). This is below the Rule-of-Three threshold for adding to a shared, framework-wide config surface. Additionally, `LLMConfig` resolution helpers are `_llm_int` and `_llm_str` — there is no `_llm_float` helper, so `overlap_conf_threshold: float` requires either a new helper (more surface proliferation) or ad-hoc inline parsing, neither of which is specified.
findings.location: Config section: '`overlap_conf_threshold` (default 0.7), `overlap_surface_cap` (default 3) on `LLMConfig`'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small `OverlapConfig` dataclass in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/config.py` with `conf_threshold: float = 0.7` and `surface_cap: int = 3`, resolved from a single `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env read in that module. Pass it explicitly to `judge()`. This avoids mutating the shared `LLMConfig` surface for a sub-feature not yet at three call-sites, and avoids the missing `_llm_float` gap entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `category: overlap` in `overlap_judge.md` with an appropriate existing value (e.g. `category: overlap-judge` as a non-review, non-criterion custom category — or simply omit it if the prompt is invoked directly by `judge.py` and never needs index selection), and confirm whether placing this file under `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` will be picked up by `_packaged_prompt_files()` and cause `build_prompt_index()` to include it.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `category: overlap` in the `overlap_judge.md` prompt front-matter, but this category value does not exist anywhere in the prompt framework. The `is_reviewer` property gates on `category == 'review'`; the plan-review criterion prompts use `category: plan-review-criterion`; all other prompts use `category: review` or ad-hoc values. A `category: overlap` value is novel and unrecognized by any existing framework routing, selection, or index-building code — it passes through silently but serves no function, making it cargo-cult use of the category field.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` (`execution_mode: single_turn`, `outputs: overlap_verdict`, `category: overlap`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Place `overlap_judge.md` in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/` (alongside `judge.py`) rather than `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/`, and load it directly by path in `judge.py`. This keeps it out of the reviewer catalog scanner entirely, avoids the novel-category problem, and co-locates the prompt with its sole consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `LLMConfig.from_env()` (src/rebar/llm/config.py) as an explicit edit target: add `_llm_float`/`_llm_int` resolution calls for `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD` → `overlap_conf_threshold` and `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP` → `overlap_surface_cap` in the Config section.
findings.finding: LLMConfig.from_env() in src/rebar/llm/config.py is an unenumerated edit target. The plan names 'config keys on LLMConfig' and the config file path, but adding overlap_conf_threshold (float) and overlap_surface_cap (int) to LLMConfig requires concrete edits inside from_env() — new _llm_int/_llm_float resolution calls for REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD and REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP — which is where the documented env-var/file-table/CLI precedence logic lives. Without this enumerated edit, an implementer may add the dataclass fields but omit the from_env() wiring, leaving the env vars inert.
findings.location: Config section: 'overlap_conf_threshold (default 0.7), overlap_surface_cap (default 3) on LLMConfig (src/rebar/llm/config.py, [tool.rebar.llm], REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Config section: 'Edit `LLMConfig` dataclass: new fields `overlap_conf_threshold: float = 0.7` and `overlap_surface_cap: int = 3`. Edit `LLMConfig.from_env()`: resolve via `_llm_float(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD", "overlap_conf_threshold", 0.7)` and `_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP", "overlap_surface_cap", 3)`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Scope section that `overlap_judge.md`'s `category: overlap` intentionally excludes it from `reviewers/index.json` (no index regen needed), or add index regeneration as a required step if the category should be `review`.
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json is an unenumerated edit target. The index is a committed file (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json) regenerated from prompt front-matter by build_prompt_index() and kept in sync by the existing test test_index_on_disk_matches_regenerated (tests/unit/test_prompt_index.py:59). Adding overlap_judge.md to the reviewers/ directory will cause that test to fail unless the plan specifies that (a) the new prompt has category != 'review' (so it is excluded from the catalog scan and the index), or (b) index.json is regenerated and committed. The plan assigns category: overlap to the new prompt but does not acknowledge the index-sync obligation.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact module and call site for `register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)` — either a new `src/rebar/llm/overlap/contracts.py` (following the code_review/plan_review pattern) or an explicit addition to the top-level `contracts.py`, and state how/when the registration is triggered.
findings.finding: The plan places the overlap_verdict contract registration at 'register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)' without naming the module where this call lives, while the existing codebase pattern for new domains is a sibling contracts module (e.g. src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py calls register_contracts() at import; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py similarly). The plan says 'register in contracts.py' but the new code lives in src/rebar/llm/overlap/judge.py — an unenumerated question of whether the call goes in the top-level contracts.py directly or in a new overlap/contracts.py (which is the established pattern for isolation).
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap_verdict schema src/rebar/schemas/overlap_verdict.schema.json + register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the established pattern: create `src/rebar/llm/overlap/contracts.py` with a `register_contracts()` function that calls `register_contract("overlap_verdict", _overlap_verdict_model)` and invoke `register_contracts()` at module import. Name this file in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the aggregation rule for directional relations (`supersedes`, `depends_on`): specify whether the rubric prompt instructs the model about ordering direction (so A→B='supersedes' and B→A='related_distinct' is the expected correct dual-ordering for a supersession), and if so update the agreement rule to handle directional-pair matching (e.g., A→B='supersedes' agrees with B→A iff B→A returns a compatible non-supersedes relation, not the same relation name).
findings.finding: The dual-ordering agreement rule ('both orderings agree on the SAME surfaceable relation') is logically inconsistent for the asymmetric `supersedes` relation: if A→B ordering emits 'supersedes' it means A supersedes B; if B→A ordering also emits 'supersedes' it means B supersedes A — both true simultaneously is a contradiction. A correctly calibrated model will therefore never produce dual-ordering agreement on 'supersedes', making it practically un-surfaceable under the stated rule. The plan needs to clarify how directional relations are handled in the aggregation step (e.g., A→B='supersedes' agrees with B→A='supersedes' only if the prompt encodes the ordering so the model knows it is judging the reverse direction — in which case the second ordering should return a DIFFERENT relation for the mirror case).
findings.location: What — output schema / aggregation rule: 'surface ONLY if both orderings agree on the SAME surfaceable relation'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the confidence aggregation rule for the surfaced result (e.g., 'take `min(confidence_A→B, confidence_B→A)` as the surfaced confidence, providing a conservative bound') and add a test case in `test_cap` that verifies ordering when pairs have differing per-ordering confidence values.
findings.finding: The surfaced result's `confidence` field is underspecified: the plan requires both orderings to independently meet `>= overlap_conf_threshold`, but does not specify how the two per-ordering confidence values are combined into the single `confidence` field of the surfaced `{counterpart_id, relation, shared_artifact, confidence, link_command}` output. Different implementers will choose min, max, or average, producing different ranking behavior for the `overlap_surface_cap` sort — and the test suite (which uses FakeRunner with canned values) may pass regardless of which rule is implemented.
findings.location: What — output schema: surface ≤ overlap_surface_cap highest-confidence results: {counterpart_id, relation, shared_artifact, confidence, link_command}
findings.suggested_fix: Use `min(confidence_A_to_B, confidence_B_to_A)` as the surfaced confidence — it is the conservative bound (both orderings must be at least this confident), is deterministic, and correctly penalizes pairs where one ordering is barely over threshold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the `test_judge_precision_recall` fixture encodes TWO canned `overlap_verdict` responses per labeled pair (one for A→B, one for B→A) so that the test exercises the dual-ordering aggregation — including cases where orderings disagree — and the precision/recall metric reflects the full pipeline behavior.
findings.finding: The precision/recall fixture test (`test_judge_precision_recall`) specifies '≥12 labeled pairs' and a FakeRunner returning 'canned per-pair overlap_verdict' — but the judge emits 2 calls per pair (both orderings). The plan does not specify that the fixture must encode both-ordering responses per pair. A fixture that only encodes a single verdict per pair (bypassing the aggregation step) would not test the dual-ordering aggregation logic, so the precision/recall metric could pass while the core mechanism remains untested.
findings.location: Testing section: 'test_judge_precision_recall (a deterministic UNIT test: a FakeRunner returns canned per-pair overlap_verdicts over a LABELED fixture of ≥12 pairs)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define the fixture as a list of `(ticket_a, ticket_b, verdict_a_to_b, verdict_b_to_a, expected_surface)` tuples; instantiate a per-call FakeRunner (or a side-effect-sequenced fake) that returns `verdict_a_to_b` for the first call and `verdict_b_to_a` for the second, then assert `expected_surface` against the judge's output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion that an LLM can emit a calibrated numeric `confidence` score: either constrain the schema to a small ordinal set (e.g., `high/medium/low` mapped to thresholds), cite a calibration experiment showing the model produces discriminative floats at 0.7, or replace with a structural gate (e.g., require explicit `shared_artifact` citation AND a categorical confidence tier) that doesn't rely on LLM numeric calibration.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `confidence: number` in `overlap_verdict` and gates all surfacing on `confidence >= overlap_conf_threshold` (default 0.7), treating an LLM's self-reported numeric confidence as a calibrated, reliable threshold signal. This is an unverified capability assertion: LLMs are systematically poorly calibrated on self-reported probability scores, and no existing rebar LLM operation uses a raw numeric confidence field as a hard gate (all prior structured outputs use categorical verdicts, severity strings, or yes/no/insufficient sub-answers). The FakeRunner-based fixture test cannot validate this because the fake returns whatever the test author wires in.
findings.location: What — output schema / Acceptance Criteria AC-3 (`overlap_conf_threshold` 0.7)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the established LLM-as-judge pairwise literature (at minimum Zheng et al. 2023) in the plan, confirm the dual-ordering rule is derived from or consistent with it, and address whether known residual failure modes (consistent sycophancy, verbose-preference bias) are out-of-scope or mitigated by prompt design in `overlap_judge.md`.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering aggregation rule (require BOTH orderings to agree on the same surfaceable relation AND each cite a non-null shared_artifact AND both confidence >= 0.7, else downgrade) is presented as the complete position-bias mitigation with no acknowledgment of the published literature on LLM-as-judge pairwise evaluation (e.g., Zheng et al. 2023 MT-Bench, which originated this dual-ordering approach). That literature also documents residual failure modes — agreement on the same wrong answer ('consistent sycophancy'), LLM preference for longer/more verbose inputs regardless of quality — that the dual-ordering rule does NOT address. The plan commits to this as THE architecture without discussing whether simpler approaches (e.g., a single-shot with Chain-of-Thought and higher threshold) or richer approaches (3-way with a tiebreaker model) were considered.
findings.location: What — AGGREGATION rule; Why section ('position-bias disagreement fails safe toward distinct')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `category: overlap` is an accepted value in the prompt loader/index builder (or document that `overlap_judge.md` must be placed OUTSIDE `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` to avoid auto-indexing), and enumerate any category-allowlist or index-rebuild step as an explicit edit target.
findings.finding: The plan places `overlap_judge.md` in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` with `category: overlap`, but `overlap` is not an existing category value in that directory. The packaged-prompt scanner (`_packaged_prompt_files()`) auto-indexes everything there into `reviewers/index.json`, which has a CI drift gate. The existing category values are `review` and `plan-review-criterion`; whether `overlap` is accepted by the prompt loader, index builder, or routing dispatch is unverified — it may silently be indexed as a reviewer or rejected.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` (`category: overlap`)
findings.suggested_fix: Move `overlap_judge.md` to a new `src/rebar/llm/overlap/` directory (parallel to the `judge.py` already scoped there) so it is not auto-scanned by the packaged-reviewer indexer, and load it directly by path in `judge.py` rather than via the prompt-library lookup. Alternatively, verify and document that `category: overlap` is added to the accepted-category set and that `index.json` regeneration is a required step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the '4-way classification' label in the Scope section with the 5-value relation enum in the What section — either rename it '5-way classification' or explicitly document which value is collapsed (e.g., related_distinct and unrelated are both 'non-surfaceable catch-all' and the prompt may treat them as one bucket).
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs that the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification,' but the What section defines a CLOSED 5-value relation enum ({duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}). No count in the plan equals four: the output enum has 5 values, the surfaceable subset has 3, and the aggregation has 2 outcomes. An implementer writing overlap_judge.md is given a self-contradictory instruction about the prompt's classification arity.
findings.location: Scope section (rubric prompt body requirement) vs. What section (output schema enum definition)
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification' → 'MUST encode: the 5-value relation classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)' and add a note that related_distinct and unrelated are both non-surfaceable and the LLM may treat them interchangeably for prompt clarity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the full dual-ordering aggregation rule (all three AND-conditions: relation agreement, non-null shared_artifact in both orderings, both confidence >= threshold) to the Scope section adjacent to the overlap_verdict schema definition, so the protocol and schema are co-located for the executor.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering aggregation protocol (both orderings must agree on the SAME surfaceable relation AND each cites a non-null shared_artifact AND both confidence >= overlap_conf_threshold; else downgrade to related_distinct) is specified only in plan prose in the What section, but is not co-located with the overlap_verdict schema definition in the Scope section or in the schema JSON. The schema itself ({relation, shared_artifact, confidence, abstain}) captures a single-ordering verdict, but the rule for combining two overlap_verdict instances into a surface decision lives orphaned in a different prose paragraph. An executor implementing judge.py must reconstruct this protocol from prose without a canonical reference adjacent to the data structure it operates on.
findings.location: What — output schema section (aggregation rule prose) vs. Scope section (overlap_verdict schema definition)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what 'the both-orderings instruction' means in the prompt body: if it means 'consider that you are receiving one of two orderings of this pair and should not be swayed by presentation order' (a reasoning bias instruction), state that explicitly; if it means something else, define it precisely so the prompt author is not left to guess.
findings.finding: The 'both-orderings instruction' is listed as something the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode', but the plan's architecture makes 2K separate judge calls (one per ordering, from judge.py code). Encoding the both-orderings instruction *inside* the per-call prompt is a pink-elephant / instruction-locality antipattern: the per-call LLM sees 'you are being called for BOTH orderings' while it is actually only processing one ordering. This conflates the caller-level orchestration protocol (call twice) with the per-call system prompt, risking prompt confusion about the LLM's own scope.
findings.location: Scope section: 'whose body MUST encode: ... the both-orderings instruction' vs. What section: 'K candidates → 2K judge calls (both orderings)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the both-orderings instruction' in the Scope MUST-encode list with: 'a position-bias awareness instruction (the LLM is told it is receiving one ordered presentation of a pair, and its verdict must not depend on presentation order)' — keeping the 2K call orchestration in judge.py code, not the prompt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'update src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json with an overlap_judge entry' to the Scope section deliverables.
findings.finding: The plan names 'src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md' as a new reviewer prompt with frontmatter (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap), but never names src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json as an edit target. The codebase confirms that index.json exists and is the canonical reviewer catalog; every other reviewer in the directory has an index.json entry. Omitting the overlap_judge entry from index.json means the reviewer will be invisible to the prompt-catalog infrastructure.
findings.location: Scope section (overlap_judge.md definition); src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json (codebase, tool-verified)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Register overlap_judge in src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json with execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap, is_reviewer: false.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
